agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier system with existing order lifecycle where 'confirmed' has an established, precise meaning. A new feature request introduces 'confirmed' status for quotes with a fundamentally different semantic meaning, creating a direct term collision in the ubiquitous language.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Quoting — these may be separate bounded contexts, but the term 'confirmed' is being reused across both with conflicting definitions, which will cause domain drift and developer confusion regardless of context boundaries.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed (Order)","definition":"Existing definition: An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. A confirmed order is immutable — no further modifications allowed."},{"term":"Confirmed (Quote — proposed)","definition":"Proposed definition: A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT yet occurred. Mutability is unspecified but implied (no payment lock)."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A pre-order artifact representing proposed terms, pricing, and service details offered to a customer."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A committed transaction in the fulfillment pipeline, progressing through statuses including 'confirmed'."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"Once an entity reaches this state, no field modifications are permitted. This is an established property of confirmed orders."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A 'confirmed' order has been paid and is queued for fulfillment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Payment receipt is a precondition for fulfillment. Violating this means fulfilling unpaid work — financial integrity is at stake.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: Every entity in 'confirmed' status within the Order context has a completed payment record."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A confirmed order is immutable — no modifications after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Immutability after payment prevents fulfillment desync (e.g., changing delivery address after dispatch is queued). This is a data consistency constraint.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: Any attempt to modify a confirmed order's fields is rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The proposed 'confirmed' quote status explicitly means payment has NOT occurred.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a proposed business policy for quote lifecycle, not a system integrity constraint. It could be renamed or restructured.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: A quote in the proposed 'confirmed' status has no associated payment record."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The term 'confirmed' must have a single unambiguous meaning within the domain's ubiquitous language.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Ubiquitous language consistency is a foundational DDD invariant. A shared term with contradictory meanings across Order and Quote contexts will cause systematic misinterpretation by developers, support staff, and automated systems (e.g., reporting queries, event handlers filtering on status='confirmed').","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: The term 'confirmed' resolves to exactly one semantic definition across all contexts where it appears in shared code, APIs, events, or reports."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been paid","when":"The order transitions to 'confirmed' status","then":"The order is queued for fulfillment and becomes immutable"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A sales agent receives verbal agreement from a customer on a quote","when":"The agent updates the quote status","then":"The quote moves to a status that clearly indicates verbal agreement without payment (status name TBD pending term resolution)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A quote is in verbally-agreed status","when":"Any system component queries entities with 'confirmed' status","then":"The quote MUST NOT appear in results intended for paid/fulfillment-ready entities"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The domain uses the term 'confirmed' for orders","when":"A new status is introduced for quotes indicating verbal agreement","then":"The new status MUST use a distinct term to avoid ubiquitous language collision"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A report or dashboard queries all 'confirmed' entities across order and quote tables","expected_behavior":"Without term disambiguation, this query conflates paid-and-immutable orders with unpaid-verbal-agreement quotes, producing misleading business metrics."},{"scenario":"An event handler listens for 'entity.confirmed' events","expected_behavior":"Handler cannot distinguish between order confirmation (trigger fulfillment) and quote confirmation (trigger follow-up for payment). Could accidentally queue unfulfilled, unpaid work."},{"scenario":"Customer support agent sees 'confirmed' status on a quote and assumes payment has been received","expected_behavior":"Support agent incorrectly tells the customer their order is being fulfilled when in reality no payment exists. Trust and operational damage."},{"scenario":"A quote is 'confirmed' (verbal agreement) and the customer disputes ever agreeing","expected_behavior":"Verbal agreement has no audit trail equivalent to a payment record. The system should not treat this with the same confidence level as a paid confirmation."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK — If 'confirmed' conflation leaks into fulfillment pipelines, the company could dispatch couriers and allocate resources for unpaid work. Verbal agreements are not payment.","operational":"HIGH RISK — Developers, support staff, and automated systems will systematically misinterpret 'confirmed' status. Every query, report, and event handler touching this term needs disambiguation. Maintenance burden compounds over time.","user":"MEDIUM RISK — Sales agents need a clear quote lifecycle status. The business need is legitimate — but the proposed term creates confusion for every other user role that already understands 'confirmed' to mean paid and immutable.","risk":"CRITICAL — This is a domain language corruption issue. The immediate feature works, but it plants a semantic landmine. Every future developer, integration, and business rule that touches 'confirmed' must now ask: 'confirmed in what sense?' This is how domain models decay.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Positive — they get a needed quote lifecycle status. Naming is cosmetic to them as long as the workflow works."},{"group":"Fulfillment/Operations Team","impact":"Negative — risk of unpaid work entering their pipeline if term collision causes logic errors."},{"group":"Finance/Billing","impact":"Negative — reporting on 'confirmed' entities could conflate paid orders with verbal agreements, corrupting revenue metrics."},{"group":"Developers","impact":"Negative — ubiquitous language violation increases cognitive load and bug surface area permanently."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Negative — status ambiguity leads to incorrect information given to customers."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is explicitly defined as paid+immutable for orders, and explicitly proposed as unpaid+verbal for quotes. Both definitions are clearly stated by stakeholders. This is not ambiguity — it is two incompatible definitions for the same term. Confidence is low because shipping this as-is guarantees domain corruption.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing definition of 'confirmed' for orders is well-established and in use","The proposed quote 'confirmed' status has a fundamentally different meaning (no payment, not immutable)","The business need for a verbal-agreement quote status is legitimate"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether quotes and orders share database tables, APIs, or event buses (affects blast radius)","Whether existing reporting or automation already filters on 'confirmed' status string"],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed' order definition is actively enforced in production code","Orders and quotes share enough infrastructure that a term collision has real technical consequences","No existing quote lifecycle statuses already cover verbal agreement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is explicitly defined for orders as 'paid and immutable' and is now proposed for quotes as 'verbally agreed, not paid.' These two definitions directly contradict each other on the payment dimension (paid vs. unpaid) and likely on the mutability dimension. Both definitions are explicitly stated by stakeholders. Using the same term for both will corrupt the ubiquitous language.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Fulfillment/Operations","Finance/Billing","Developers","Customer Support"],"options":["A) Rename the quote status to 'customer_accepted' or 'verbally_agreed' — preserves existing 'confirmed' semantics for orders, gives quotes a precise term","B) Rename the quote status to 'pending_payment' — emphasizes what is missing rather than what happened","C) Use 'confirmed' for both but add a qualifier (e.g., 'quote_confirmed' vs 'order_confirmed') — partial fix, still risks collision in shared infrastructure","D) Proceed as-is with 'confirmed' for both — NOT RECOMMENDED, guarantees domain language corruption"],"recommendation":"Option A: Use 'customer_accepted' for quotes. It precisely describes what happened (customer accepted terms), clearly distinguishes from order confirmation (which requires payment), and is self-documenting for all stakeholder groups. The business need is real — only the naming is wrong."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' cannot simultaneously mean 'paid and immutable' (orders) and 'verbally agreed, not paid' (quotes)","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 — The term 'confirmed' must have a single unambiguous meaning within the domain's ubiquitous language"},{"violation":"Risk of unpaid fulfillment: if the term collision causes any system component to treat a 'confirmed' quote as a 'confirmed' order, work may be dispatched without payment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 — A 'confirmed' order has been paid and is queued for fulfillment"}]}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this feature request carefully. There's a clear term collision: "confirmed" is already defined in the domain for orders (paid, queued for fulfillment, immutable), and now they want to use "confirmed" for quotes with a completely different meaning (verbal agreement, no payment, presumably still mutable).

This is a contradiction - two explicit definitions for the same term in the same domain that mean different things and cannot both be satisfied simultaneously.

Let me produce the analysis.

This is an established property of confirmed orders."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A 'confirmed' order has been paid and is queued for fulfillment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Payment receipt is a precondition for fulfillment. A shared term with contradictory meanings across Order and Quote contexts will cause systematic misinterpretation by developers, support staff, and automated systems (e.g., reporting queries, event handlers filtering on status='confirmed').","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: The term 'confirmed' resolves to exactly one semantic definition across all contexts where it appears in shared code, APIs, events, or reports."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been paid","when":"The order transitions to 'confirmed' status","then":"The order is queued for fulfillment and becomes immutable"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A sales agent receives verbal agreement from a customer on a quote","when":"The agent updates the quote status","then":"The quote moves to a status that clearly indicates verbal agreement without payment (status name TBD pending term resolution)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A quote is in verbally-agreed status","when":"Any system component queries entities with 'confirmed' status","then":"The quote MUST NOT appear in results intended for paid/fulfillment-ready entities"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The domain uses the term 'confirmed' for orders","when":"A new status is introduced for quotes indicating verbal agreement","then":"The new status MUST use a distinct term to avoid ubiquitous language collision"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A report or dashboard queries all 'confirmed' entities across order and quote tables","expected_behavior":"Without term disambiguation, this query conflates paid-and-immutable orders with unpaid-verbal-agreement quotes, producing misleading business metrics."},{"scenario":"An event handler listens for 'entity.confirmed' events","expected_behavior":"Handler cannot distinguish between order confirmation (trigger fulfillment) and quote confirmation (trigger follow-up for payment). Using the same term for both will corrupt the ubiquitous language.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Fulfillment/Operations","Finance/Billing","Developers","Customer Support"],"options":["A) Rename the quote status to 'customer_accepted' or 'verbally_agreed' — preserves existing 'confirmed' semantics for orders, gives quotes a precise term","B) Rename the quote status to 'pending_payment' — emphasizes what is missing rather than what happened","C) Use 'confirmed' for both but add a qualifier (e.g., 'quote_confirmed' vs 'order_confirmed') — partial fix, still risks collision in shared infrastructure","D) Proceed as-is with 'confirmed' for both — NOT RECOMMENDED, guarantees domain language corruption"],"recommendation":"Option A: Use 'customer_accepted' for quotes. It precisely describes what happened (customer accepted terms), clearly distinguishes from order confirmation (which requires payment), and is self-documenting for all stakeholder groups. The business need is real — only the naming is wrong."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' cannot simultaneously mean 'paid and immutable' (orders) and 'verbally agreed, not paid' (quotes)","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 — The term 'confirmed' must have a single unambiguous meaning within the domain's ubiquitous language"},{"violation":"Risk of unpaid fulfillment: if the term collision causes any system component to treat a 'confirmed' quote as a 'confirmed' order, work may be dispatched without payment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 — A 'confirmed' order has been paid and is queued for fulfillment"}]}
